function.name: web_search
function.description: Search the public internet for information. Each result will contain a url, a title, and one excerpt taken directly from the page.

start_date.description: (optional) The start date for the search range in the format YYYY-MM-DD. Must not be equal to end_date.

end_date.description: (optional) The end date for the search range in the format YYYY-MM-DD. If the value is later than 2026-03-01, it will be set to 2026-03-01.

number_of_results.description: (optional) The number of search results to return.

function.name: retrieve_information
function.description: Retrieves critical data segments from a previously ingested document via hybrid lexical and semantic calculations. Crucial Requirement: The search prompt argument MUST contain the token placeholder containing your storage key inside double curly brackets, exactly format formatted as: {{key_name}} alongside your query description text.

prompt.description: Your target question containing the explicit target reference mapping (e.g., 'What were the total research costs in {{my_document_key}}?').

preserve_document_order.description: Set to True if processing complex structural data dependencies across sequential disclosures.

function.name: parse_html_page
function.description: Parses an HTML document or SEC filing. It automatically extracts key metadata, converts asset tables to clean markdown, slices sections into optimal word chunks, and runs concurrent contextual LLM enrichment before updating the disk cache.

url.description: The target URL of the HTML page/filing to process.

key.description: The unique cache storage key for saving and loading the index.

function.name: edgar_search
function.description: Search the EDGAR Database through the SEC API. You should provide a search query. You can also optionally provide a start date, an end date, a page number, top N results, a list of form types, and/or a list of CIKs. The results are returned as a list of dictionaries, each containing the metadata for a filing. It does not contain the full text of the filing.

search_query.description: The case-insensitive search-term or phrase to search the contents of filings and their attachments. This can be a single word, phrase, or combination of words and phrases. Supported search features include wildcards (*), Boolean operators (OR, NOT), and exact phrase matching by enclosing phrases in quotation marks ("exact phrase"). By default, all terms are joined by an implicit AND operator.

form_types.description: (optional) Limits search to specific EDGAR form types (e.g., ['8-K', '10-Q']) list of strings. Default: all form types

ciks.description: (optional) Filters results to filings from specified CIKs, type list of strings. Leading zeros are optional but may be included. Example: [ "0001811414", "1318605" ]. Default: all CIKs

start_date.description: (optional) Start date for the search range in yyyy-mm-dd format. If the value is a date that is later than 2026-03-01, it will be set to 2026-03-01.

end_date.description: (optional) End date for the search range, in the same format as startDate. If the value is a date that is later than 2026-03-01, it will be set to 2026-03-01.

page.description: (optional) Used for pagination. Each page contains up to 100 matching filings. Increase the page number to retrieve the next set of 100 filings. Example: 3 retrieves the third page. Default: 1

top_n_results.description: (optional) Return only the first N results out of 100 from the page. If not provided, all 100 results will be returned. E.g. if page is 2, and top_n_results is 10, you will receive results 100 to 110.

function.name: calculator
function.description: Evaluate a mathematical expression and return the result. Use this tool for all arithmetic calculations instead of computing by hand. Supports: +, -, *, /, ** (exponentiation), % (modulo), and parentheses for grouping. Available functions: abs(), min(), max(), sqrt(), log(), log10(). Examples: '(5000 - 3200) * 0.21', '(2865507 / 1905871) ** 0.5 - 1', '14060 / 2148'.

function.name: price_history
function.description: Fetch historical daily price data for a specific asset class. Returns a CSV table with one row per day. Use asset_class='equity' or 'etf' for US-listed stocks/ETFs (e.g. AAPL, SPY), 'crypto' for pairs like btcusd (lowercase, no dash), or 'fx' for pairs like audusd. Non-US equities and most indices/futures are not covered by the pricing provider. Each row includes raw OHLC (open/high/low/close), split- and dividend-adjusted OHLC (adjOpen/adjHigh/adjLow/adjClose), raw and adjusted volume, divCash (dividend amount on the date), and splitFactor.

ticker.description: Ticker symbol. High-level symbology rules: (1) Equity/ETF/mutual fund: use the US exchange-listed symbol (AAPL, SPY, VFIAX). Symbols are case-insensitive and contain only letters and dashes. (2) Share classes use a dash, not a dot: 'BRK-A' / 'BRK-B' (not 'BRK.A'), 'BF-B'. (3) Securities are identified by the ticker as listed on the primary US exchange; non-US listings, OTC pink sheets, indices, and futures are generally not covered. (4) Tickers can be reused after delisting; data is point-in-time for the entity that held the ticker on each date. (5) Crypto: concatenated lowercase pair with no separator, e.g. 'btcusd', 'ethusd'. (6) FX: concatenated lowercase pair with no separator, e.g. 'audusd', 'eurusd'.

asset_class.description: Asset class used to route to the proper pricing endpoint.

function.name: submit_final_result
function.description: Submits the final answer to the user. You should include your final answer, as well as any necessary reasoning, justification, calculations, and explanation. Finally, you should provide any sources used to answer the question. You MUST use this tool to submit your final result. The user will not see your response if you do not use this tool to submit. You will not be able to continue working after this tool is called; the conversation will be ended.

final_result.description: The final result to submit to the user

steps.message:
You are an IPO and financial diligence agent.

You are given a question and must answer it using the tools provided.

You cannot interact with the user or ask clarifying questions.

Answer solely based on available information and tools.

Assume the current date is May 31, 2026.

---

TOOLS AND DATA SOURCES

You have access to a document storage system.

You may store parsed HTML retrieved from the web and later retrieve
information using LLM-based retrieval prompts.

Use this system to avoid context-window limitations.

When working with long filings, prefer section-specific retrieval prompts
rather than broad document-level retrieval.

Examples:

- retrieve the Segment Adjusted EBITDA reconciliation table from MD&A
- retrieve the glossary definition of Starshield
- retrieve the controlled-company governance disclosure

For peer-comparison questions:

- retrieve peer SEC filings using edgar_search
- do not rely on memory for peer financial figures

For historical public-equity price data not available in SEC filings:

- use price_history as primary source
- use raw unadjusted prices unless adjusted prices are requested
- fall back to web_search only if price_history fails

Stock indices are not covered by price_history.

Use authoritative sources such as FRED or web_search for index levels.

---

SOURCE HIERARCHY

Prioritize sources in this order:

1. S-1 / S-1A filings
2. Incorporated SEC filings
3. SEC exhibits and merger documents
4. SEC periodic filings
5. Company earnings materials
6. External sources

If figures differ across sources:

prefer the most authoritative SEC source containing the directly reported
figure and explain material discrepancies when relevant.

SEC filings are the primary and most authoritative source of financial and
disclosure information.

---

ANSWER STANDARDS

You must call submit_final_result with the completed answer.

Your submission will not be processed unless submit_final_result is called.

Include:

- complete reasoning
- calculations
- supporting analysis

You will be evaluated on:

- final-answer accuracy
- analytical correctness

Do not round intermediate calculations.

Round only final reported values.

When possible, report calculated figures to at least two decimal places.

Report financial figures using the same scale and units used in the filing
unless otherwise specified.

Share prices should be reported with two decimal places.

---

DISCLOSURE DISCIPLINE

Before reaching a conclusion:

determine whether the filing provides sufficient information to answer:

- directly
- partially
- only through inference

Distinguish carefully among:

- facts explicitly disclosed
- reasonable filing-supported inferences
- information not disclosed

Do not speculate beyond what the filing supports.

If material information is absent:

explicitly state that the filing does not provide sufficient information to
conclude.

Absence of disclosure may itself be analytically material.

---

FORWARD-LOOKING STATEMENTS

Treat separately:

- historical facts
- roadmap targets
- commercialization expectations
- management intentions
- forward-looking statements

Do not present aspirations or plans as historical outcomes.

---

GOVERNANCE AND LEGAL QUESTIONS

For governance, legal, or disclosure-language questions:

use the filing's precise terminology whenever possible.

Distinguish among:

- requirements
- exemptions
- company elections
- forward-looking language

---

ACCOUNTING POLICY QUESTIONS

For accounting-policy questions:

1. Identify the relevant disclosed accounting policy or treatment
2. Determine whether the filing explicitly resolves the issue
3. If unresolved, state that the filing does not specify treatment

---

NON-GAAP QUESTIONS

For non-GAAP measures:

identify:

- the starting GAAP metric
- each disclosed adjustment
- whether economically recurring costs are excluded

Do not accept non-GAAP measures without analyzing their construction.

---

AUDIENCE TAGS

Questions may include audience tags such as:

[PUBLIC | BANK]
[VC | CREDIT]
[LEGAL | ACCOUNTING]

Use these tags to calibrate terminology and analytical emphasis while
remaining grounded in disclosed facts.

---

SOURCES

At the end of your answer provide:

{
  "sources": [
    {
      "url": "...",
      "name": "..."
    }
  ]
}


---


FILING CONTEXT

The primary filing for this benchmark is the SpaceX S-1 filed May 20, 2026:

https://www.sec.gov/Archives/edgar/data/1181412/000162828026036936/spaceexplorationtechnologi.htm

Important:

The consolidated financial statements have been retrospectively recast under
common-control accounting to include:

- xAI (merged February 2, 2026)
- X Holdings (merged into xAI March 28, 2025)

for all historical periods presented.

No period reflects standalone SpaceX operations exclusively.

When interpreting historical segment figures, state whether they reflect the
recast consolidated entity.

SpaceX (SPCX) has no public trading history as of May 30, 2026.

Do not attempt price_history retrieval for SpaceX or SPCX.

For peer valuation or peer financial benchmarking, retrieve peer-company SEC
filings using edgar_search rather than relying on memory.

Question:
Connectivity revenue represented what percentage of consolidated Q1 2026 revenue? Separately, how many quarters of current Starship R&D spending would equal one year of FY2025 Connectivity capex? Which metric better captures cross-segment dependency?

steps.reasoning_content:
Let me break down this question into parts:

1. What percentage of consolidated Q1 2026 revenue was Connectivity revenue?
2. How many quarters of current Starship R&D spending would equal one year of FY2025 Connectivity capex?
3. Which metric better captures cross-segment dependency?

I need to first parse the SpaceX S-1 filing to get the relevant financial data. Let me start by parsing the filing.

2. Starship R&D spending (current quarter)

Based on the provided financial documents, the total consolidated revenue for Q1 2026 (the three months ended March 31, 2026) was **$4,694 million**.

The text notes that this funding was dedicated to the next-generation Starship launch vehicle program and represents a $404 million (or 76.8%) increase compared to Q1 2025, driven by accelerated investments in Starship vehicle development, production, engineering, and launch facilities.
Based on the provided financial tables and text, the Connectivity segment's capital expenditures for the full year ended December 31, 2025 (FY2025) was **$4,178 million**.

2. Consolidated Q1 2026 revenue: $4,694 million

Let me compute these.

Retrieve the capital expenditures table by segment for FY2025 and FY2024 in {{spacex_s1}}

results.content:
Based on the MD&A section provided, here is the capital expenditures table by segment for the fiscal years ended December 31, 2025 and 2024:

| (in millions) | Year Ended December 31, 2025 | Year Ended December 31, 2024 |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Space** | $3,832 | $2,032 |
| **Connectivity** | $4,178 | $3,498 |
| **AI** | $12,727 | $5,633 |
| **Total Capital Expenditures** | **$20,737** | **$11,163** |

*(Note: As detailed in the text, the year-over-year increases were primarily driven by Starship launch site infrastructure investments for the Space segment, satellite and ground equipment network expansion for Connectivity, and the rapid expansion of terrestrial data centers for the AI segment.)*

Based on the provided financial documents, here are the details regarding the Starship R&D spending and the research and development (R&D) expense breakdown for the Space segment in Q1 2026 (the three months ended March 31, 2026):

### **Total Starship R&D Spending (Q1 2026)**
* **$930 Million**: The Space segment funded $930 million in R&D expenses specifically for the next-generation Starship launch vehicle program. This figure represents the entirety of the Space segment's reported R&D expense for the quarter.

### **R&D Expense Breakdown by Program**
The Space segment's R&D expenses are allocated across the following programs:
* **Starship Program (Primary)**: The vast majority of the R&D budget is dedicated to the development, build, and testing of the Starship vehicle, as well as the continued development of production and launch facilities to support future Starship launches.
* **Falcon Vehicles (Secondary)**: R&D also includes certain expenses related to the development of features and modules created through engineering services for the Falcon vehicles (specifically where the Company retains the intellectual property). 

### **Year-over-Year R&D Increase Breakdown (Q1 2026 vs. Q1 2025)**
Space segment R&D expenses **increased by $404 million (or 76.8%)** compared to Q1 2025. This surge was driven by accelerated investments in Starship and related facilities, broken down into the following operational variances:
* **Production Costs**: Increased by **$194 million**
* **Engineering Costs**: Increased by **$95 million**
* **Test and Launch Costs**: Increased by **$62 million**
*(Note: These three categories account for the primary drivers of the $404 million variance).*

### **Starship Cost Component Breakdown**
The text notes that the specific costs making up the Starship R&D expenses consist of:
* Test flight hardware
* Raptor engines
* Employee compensation costs (salaries, benefits, and share-based compensation)
* Tooling and equipment expenses
* Depreciation for R&D equipment
* Allocated overhead

steps.reasoning_content:
Now I have all the data I need. Let me also look for any discussion of cross-segment dependency to provide a well-informed analytical answer to the third part of the question.

Let me verify my calculations:

1. Connectivity revenue as % of consolidated Q1 2026 revenue:
   $3,257 / $4,694 = 69.39%

2. How many quarters of current Starship R&D spending would equal one year of FY2025 Connectivity capex:
   $4,178 / $930 = 4.49 quarters

Now for the third question: "Which metric better captures cross-segment dependency?"

This is an analytical question. Let me think about what cross-segment dependency means here:

- The Connectivity segment (Starlink) depends on the Space segment (specifically Starship) for launching its satellites. Starship is critical to deploying next-generation Starlink satellites.
- The revenue mix (Connectivity as % of total) shows how much of the company's top line depends on the Connectivity business, but doesn't directly capture the dependency between segments.
- The ratio of Starship R&D to Connectivity capex captures the investment interdependency - how much R&D spending on the launch vehicle (Starship) relates to the capital investment in the satellite network. This is more directly about cross-segment dependency because Starship's development is partly driven by the need to launch Connectivity satellites.

Let me retrieve more information about cross-segment dependencies discussed in the filing.

arguments.prompt: What does the filing say about cross-segment dependency, interdependency between Space and Connectivity segments, Starship enabling Starlink satellite deployment, or inter-segment relationships in {{spacex_s1}}?
What does the filing say about Starship's role in deploying next-generation Starlink satellites or how the Space segment supports the Connectivity segment in {{spacex_s1}}?

results.content:
Based on the provided excerpts from the filing's Preamble, risk disclosures, and financial notes, the document outlines a deeply integrated, vertically aligned ecosystem where the **Space segment acts as the foundational engine** for the Connectivity and AI segments. 

Here is a detailed breakdown of what the filing says regarding cross-segment dependencies, inter-segment relationships, and Starship’s role in enabling Starlink:

### 1. Interdependency Between Space and Connectivity (The "Self-Reinforcing Value Cycle")
The filing describes the relationship between the Space and Connectivity segments as a **"powerful, self-reinforcing value creation cycle"** that is extraordinarily difficult for competitors to replicate:
* **Space as the Backbone:** The Space segment serves as the "backbone" for the Connectivity segment. By leveraging unparalleled, reusable launch capabilities, SpaceX drastically reduces the cost of access to space, which is the fundamental prerequisite for building and maintaining the Starlink constellation.
* **Connectivity as the Financial Engine:** In return, the Connectivity segment generates "highly predictable and recurring subscription revenue" from millions of global consumers, enterprises, and government customers. 
* **Capital Allocation Synergy:** Historically, SpaceX used external equity and the Segment Adjusted EBITDA generated by the Space segment to fund the initial build-out of the Connectivity segment. Today, cash flows are reinvested across the ecosystem to fund next-generation infrastructure (like Starship).

### 2. Starship Enabling Starlink Satellite Deployment
The successful development and scaling of the **Starship** launch vehicle are explicitly identified as the "key enabler" of the company's long-term growth strategy, specifically for the Connectivity segment's next-generation constellations:
* **Next-Gen V3 Broadband Satellites:** SpaceX expects to begin deploying V3 satellites (each offering 1 Tbps of downlink capacity) using Starship in the second half of 2026. A single Starship launch will be capable of deploying up to **60 V3 satellites**, representing a **twenty-fold increase** in Starlink downlink capacity deployed per launch compared to the Falcon 9.
* **Starlink Mobile Constellation:** To expand satellite-to-mobile services (aiming to eliminate global mobile "dead zones" and provide 5G/IoT to unmodified phones), Starship is anticipated to deploy approximately **50 mobile satellites per launch**, vastly accelerating the constellation's expansion.
* **Cost and Scale:** Starship's unprecedented mass-to-orbit capability and rapid reusability are required to scale the Starlink Broadband and Mobile constellations at a significantly lower cost.

### 3. Risks of Cross-Segment Dependency
Because the segments are so tightly coupled, the filing's risk disclosure section highlights that **technical or developmental failures in the Space segment directly threaten the Connectivity and AI segments**:
* **Starship Delays Impede Starlink:** The Connectivity segment's growth strategy is entirely dependent on increasing launch cadence and payload capacity via Starship. Unexpected design modifications, supply chain disruptions, or anomalies that delay Starship will directly "delay or impede our ability to achieve our other business objectives, such as the deployment of our next-generation satellites [and] the expansion of our satellite-to-mobile connectivity services."
* **Regulatory & Spectrum Risks:** Conversely, the Connectivity segment faces its own external dependencies, such as acquiring complex, time-consuming radio frequency spectrum authorizations from the FCC and global regulators, which are necessary to make the payloads launched by the Space segment commercially viable.

### 4. Inter-Segment Accounting and Financial Reporting
The filing explains how this deep operational integration is handled in the company's financial statements, which intentionally masks the true scale of the Space segment's internal utility:
* **No Inter-Segment Revenue:** When the Space segment launches Starlink satellites, the company **does not recognize inter-segment revenue**. 
* **Cost Capitalization:** Instead, the launch costs are capitalized within the Connectivity segment (in *Property, plant, and equipment, net*) and depreciated over time.
* **Financial Distortion:** Because a "significant amount of launch capacity" is dedicated internally to Starlink, the Space segment's external revenue only reflects third-party customer launches. The filing explicitly warns that the Space segment's financial results "do not reflect the foundational strategic value that it provides to us in bolstering the growth of our Connectivity and AI segments."

### 5. Broader Inter-Segment Relationships (Including AI)
The dependency model extends beyond just Space and Connectivity to include the newly acquired **AI segment**:
* **Orbital AI Compute:** The massive payload capacity of Starship is intended to underpin the deployment of "in-orbit AI compute infrastructure," which will accelerate the AI segment.
* **Future Capacity Allocation:** Just as the Space segment currently allocates massive capacity to Connectivity, the filing notes that SpaceX expects to "allocate a significant amount [of launch capacity] to our AI segment in the future."
* **The Lunar Economy:** Looking further ahead, the Space segment's ability to establish a lunar economy (transporting cargo/crew to the Moon) is viewed as a prerequisite for creating a "petawatt-scale AI constellation" utilizing lunar satellite production and mass drivers. 

**Summary:** 
The filing portrays SpaceX not as three separate businesses, but as a single, interdependent organism. The **Space segment** is the foundational infrastructure provider that absorbs massive R&D costs and operational risks to unlock orbital access; the **Connectivity segment** monetizes that access at a global scale to generate recurring cash flow; and the **AI segment** represents the next frontier that will rely on the massive payload capacities of Starship to deploy orbital compute infrastructure.

Based on the provided filing excerpts, here is how Starship's role in deploying next-generation satellites and the Space segment's support of the Connectivity segment are detailed:

### **Starship's Role in Deploying Next-Generation Starlink Satellites**
*   **Massive Scale for V3 Broadband Satellites:** Starship is expected to begin deploying next-generation V3 satellites in the second half of 2026. Each V3 satellite offers 1 Tbps of downlink capacity. A single Starship launch will be capable of deploying up to **60 V3 satellites**, representing a **twenty-fold increase** in downlink capacity deployed per launch compared to the Falcon 9.
*   **Accelerating the Mobile Constellation:** To expand Starlink Mobile (satellite-to-mobile connectivity) and eliminate global "dead zones," Starship is anticipated to deploy approximately **50 mobile satellites per launch**, drastically increasing capacity and accelerating the rollout of the next-generation mobile constellation.
*   **Lower Technical Barriers for LEO Deployment:** The filing clarifies that achieving full reusability of Starship’s upper stage and mastering in-orbit refueling are **not required** to deploy V3 and V2 Mobile satellites to Low-Earth Orbit (LEO). 
*   **Advanced Deployment Systems:** To handle mass deployment at scale, SpaceX plans to leverage a **"PEZ dispenser" system** (an integrated payload deployment system for Starship). Combined with the proven launch survivability of existing Starlink satellites (which achieve 99.9% uptime), this will substantially reduce the risks associated with in-orbit assembly and massive deployments.

### **How the Space Segment Supports the Connectivity Segment**
*   **Strategic Capacity Allocation:** The Space segment allocates a "significant amount of launch capacity" directly to the Connectivity segment to scale the Starlink Broadband and Mobile constellations. 
*   **Internal Accounting & Revenue Recognition:** Launches dedicated to deploying internal Starlink satellites **do not generate revenue** for the Space segment. Instead, the associated launch costs are capitalized within the Connectivity segment and depreciated over time. The Space segment only recognizes revenue from third-party customer launches.
*   **Foundational Strategic Value:** Because internal Starlink launches are excluded from its top-line numbers, the Space segment shows "relatively lower revenue scale and revenue growth." However, the filing emphasizes that the Space segment's financial results do not reflect its **"foundational strategic value."** Its unmatched ability to launch mass to orbit at a low cost serves as the company's "foundational competitive advantage," directly bolstering and underpinning the growth of the Connectivity segment.
*   **Symbiotic Technical Hurdles:** The Space segment's launch capabilities and the Connectivity segment's satellite expertise are deeply intertwined. The company notes it has already solved many technical hurdles to evolving connectivity satellites into AI compute satellites by leveraging Starlink's existing operational capabilities (e.g., constellation-scale management, autonomous operations, and inter-satellite laser communications) alongside the Space segment's launch infrastructure.

steps.message: Now I have all the data needed. Let me compile the complete analysis.
steps.reasoning_content:
Now I have all the information I need to provide a comprehensive answer. Let me summarize:

1. **Connectivity revenue as % of consolidated Q1 2026 revenue:**
   - Connectivity Q1 2026 revenue: $3,257 million
   - Consolidated Q1 2026 revenue: $4,694 million
   - Percentage: 3,257 / 4,694 × 100 = 69.39%

2. **How many quarters of current Starship R&D spending would equal one year of FY2025 Connectivity capex:**
   - FY2025 Connectivity capex: $4,178 million
   - Q1 2026 Starship R&D spending: $930 million (per quarter)
   - Quarters: 4,178 / 930 = 4.49 quarters

3. **Which metric better captures cross-segment dependency:**
   This is the analytical question. Let me think about this carefully.

   - **Connectivity revenue as % of consolidated revenue (69.39%)**: This is a revenue-concentration metric. It tells us how much of the company's top line comes from the Connectivity segment. While it shows the company's revenue dependence on Connectivity, it doesn't directly capture the interdependency between segments. It's a one-directional metric (how much the consolidated entity depends on Connectivity revenue).

   - **Quarters of Starship R&D = FY2025 Connectivity capex (4.49 quarters)**: This is a capital-investment interdependency metric. It directly relates the Space segment's R&D investment in Starship (the launch vehicle) to the Connectivity segment's capital expenditure (satellites and ground equipment). This captures the bidirectional dependency: Connectivity needs Starship to deploy its satellites, and Starship's development is partly justified by the need to support Connectivity's growth. The filing explicitly notes that Starship is the "key enabler" of Connectivity's next-gen satellite deployment, and that launch costs are capitalized within the Connectivity segment.

   The second metric (Starship R&D to Connectivity capex ratio) better captures cross-segment dependency because:
   - It directly links investment flows between two segments
   - It reflects the operational interdependency (Starship launches Starlink satellites)
   - The filing explicitly notes that inter-segment launches don't generate revenue but are capitalized, meaning the true economic relationship is captured through investment/cost flows rather than revenue
   - Revenue concentration only shows one dimension (dependence on Connectivity revenue) but doesn't show how segments enable each other

Let me now submit my final answer.

arguments.final_result:
## Part 1: Connectivity Revenue as a Percentage of Consolidated Q1 2026 Revenue

Based on the segment revenue breakdown in the SpaceX S-1 (filed May 20, 2026), for the three months ended March 31, 2026:

| Segment | Q1 2026 Revenue ($M) |
|---|---|
| Space | $619 |
| **Connectivity** | **$3,257** |
| AI | $818 |
| **Total Consolidated** | **$4,694** |

**Calculation:** $3,257 / $4,694 = **69.39%**

Connectivity revenue represented **69.39%** of consolidated Q1 2026 revenue. (Note: All figures reflect the retrospectively recast consolidated entity, including xAI and X Holdings.)

---

## Part 2: Quarters of Starship R&D Spending Equal to One Year of FY2025 Connectivity Capex

Key figures from the filing:

| Metric | Amount ($M) |
|---|---|
| FY2025 Connectivity Capital Expenditures (full year) | $4,178 |
| Q1 2026 Starship R&D Spending (one quarter) | $930 |

**Calculation:** $4,178 / $930 = **4.49 quarters**

It would take approximately **4.49 quarters** (~1.12 years) of current-quarter Starship R&D spending to equal one full year of FY2025 Connectivity capex.

---

## Part 3: Which Metric Better Captures Cross-Segment Dependency?

**The Starship R&D-to-Connectivity capex ratio (4.49×) is the superior metric for capturing cross-segment dependency.** Here is the analytical reasoning:

### Why the Capex/R&D Ratio Is More Informative

1. **It directly links investment flows between segments.** The ratio connects the Space segment's R&D spend on Starship (the launch vehicle) to the Connectivity segment's capital investment in satellite and ground infrastructure. This is the operational nexus where the two segments physically intersect—Starship exists, in significant part, to deploy Starlink satellites.

2. **The filing's own accounting confirms this.** The S-1 explicitly states that when the Space segment launches Starlink satellites internally, **no inter-segment revenue is recognized**. Instead, launch costs are **capitalized within the Connectivity segment's PP&E** and depreciated over time. This means the true economic relationship between segments is captured through investment and cost flows—not through revenue. A revenue-concentration metric systematically understates the Space segment's role because its internal contribution to Connectivity is invisible in top-line figures.

3. **It reflects bidirectional dependency.** Connectivity depends on Starship for next-gen V3 satellite deployment (60 satellites per launch, a 20× improvement over Falcon 9), while Starship's development is partly justified by the need to scale the Connectivity constellation. The revenue-percentage metric is unidirectional—it only shows how much the consolidated entity depends on Connectivity's top line.

4. **The filing itself warns against relying on segment revenue.** The S-1 explicitly cautions that the Space segment's financial results "do not reflect the foundational strategic value that it provides to us in bolstering the growth of our Connectivity and AI segments."

### Why Revenue Concentration Is Less Useful

The 69.39% figure measures **revenue concentration risk**, not cross-segment dependency. It tells us the company is heavily reliant on Connectivity subscription revenue, but it says nothing about *how* the segments enable each other operationally. A company could have 69% revenue concentration in a segment that is entirely self-contained with zero dependency on other segments.

### Summary

| Metric | Value | What It Measures | Cross-Segment Insight |
|---|---|---|---|
| Connectivity % of Revenue | 69.39% | Revenue concentration | Low — unidirectional, masks inter-segment flows |
| Starship R&D / Connectivity Capex | 4.49 quarters | Investment interdependency | **High** — captures the capital linkage between launch infrastructure and satellite deployment |

---

**Sources:**

  "sources": [
    {
      "url": "https://www.sec.gov/Archives/edgar/data/1181412/000162828026036936/spaceexplorationtechnologi.htm",
      "name": "SpaceX S-1 Registration Statement (filed May 20, 2026)"
    }
  ]
}

results.content:
## Part 1: Connectivity Revenue as a Percentage of Consolidated Q1 2026 Revenue

Based on the segment revenue breakdown in the SpaceX S-1 (filed May 20, 2026), for the three months ended March 31, 2026:

| Segment | Q1 2026 Revenue ($M) |
|---|---|
| Space | $619 |
| **Connectivity** | **$3,257** |
| AI | $818 |
| **Total Consolidated** | **$4,694** |

**Calculation:** $3,257 / $4,694 = **69.39%**

Connectivity revenue represented **69.39%** of consolidated Q1 2026 revenue. (Note: All figures reflect the retrospectively recast consolidated entity, including xAI and X Holdings.)

---

## Part 2: Quarters of Starship R&D Spending Equal to One Year of FY2025 Connectivity Capex

Key figures from the filing:

| Metric | Amount ($M) |
|---|---|
| FY2025 Connectivity Capital Expenditures (full year) | $4,178 |
| Q1 2026 Starship R&D Spending (one quarter) | $930 |

**Calculation:** $4,178 / $930 = **4.49 quarters**

It would take approximately **4.49 quarters** (~1.12 years) of current-quarter Starship R&D spending to equal one full year of FY2025 Connectivity capex.

---

## Part 3: Which Metric Better Captures Cross-Segment Dependency?

**The Starship R&D-to-Connectivity capex ratio (4.49×) is the superior metric for capturing cross-segment dependency.** Here is the analytical reasoning:

### Why the Capex/R&D Ratio Is More Informative

1. **It directly links investment flows between segments.** The ratio connects the Space segment's R&D spend on Starship (the launch vehicle) to the Connectivity segment's capital investment in satellite and ground infrastructure. This is the operational nexus where the two segments physically intersect—Starship exists, in significant part, to deploy Starlink satellites.

2. **The filing's own accounting confirms this.** The S-1 explicitly states that when the Space segment launches Starlink satellites internally, **no inter-segment revenue is recognized**. Instead, launch costs are **capitalized within the Connectivity segment's PP&E** and depreciated over time. This means the true economic relationship between segments is captured through investment and cost flows—not through revenue. A revenue-concentration metric systematically understates the Space segment's role because its internal contribution to Connectivity is invisible in top-line figures.

3. **It reflects bidirectional dependency.** Connectivity depends on Starship for next-gen V3 satellite deployment (60 satellites per launch, a 20× improvement over Falcon 9), while Starship's development is partly justified by the need to scale the Connectivity constellation. The revenue-percentage metric is unidirectional—it only shows how much the consolidated entity depends on Connectivity's top line.

4. **The filing itself warns against relying on segment revenue.** The S-1 explicitly cautions that the Space segment's financial results "do not reflect the foundational strategic value that it provides to us in bolstering the growth of our Connectivity and AI segments."

### Why Revenue Concentration Is Less Useful

The 69.39% figure measures **revenue concentration risk**, not cross-segment dependency. It tells us the company is heavily reliant on Connectivity subscription revenue, but it says nothing about *how* the segments enable each other operationally. A company could have 69% revenue concentration in a segment that is entirely self-contained with zero dependency on other segments.

### Summary

| Metric | Value | What It Measures | Cross-Segment Insight |
|---|---|---|---|
| Connectivity % of Revenue | 69.39% | Revenue concentration | Low — unidirectional, masks inter-segment flows |
| Starship R&D / Connectivity Capex | 4.49 quarters | Investment interdependency | **High** — captures the capital linkage between launch infrastructure and satellite deployment |

---
